sentences.text: No, [?
Tobins-- ?] [?
Tobins ?] was the place my wife was working, and his factory was a couple blocks away from the building, on the outside of the ghetto.
My daughter--
My daughter-- as I said, she was-- before that, she wasn't working.
She wasn't with my wife in the [?
Tobins ?] factory.
My wife stopped working in the-- well, in the ghetto.
Oh, when she went away to the Polish people?
Eight days before the uprising.
One-- a friend came in and said, I could save your kid, your girl, and we agreed to it, and the Polish people--
He was a guy who was going-- going out and in the ghetto, bringing stuff and selling stuff, you know, taking out stuff from the ghetto and selling it on the Polish side.
No, not-- no, I don't remember his name.
It was a Jewish guy, and he said, I think I can [INAUDIBLE] Polish people came.
The women-- the Polish women came, and she took the girl, my baby, away.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Jamiolkowsky is the name.
Yes.
J-A-M-I-O-L-K-O-W-S-K-Y, Jamiolkowsky.
Janina Jamiolkowsky, her name.
No, without money.
We just put together some clothing for the baby.
She was 2 and 1/2 years old, and we gave her the clothing.
That's all she got.
Because Warsaw had a lot of Jews.
Half a million Jews lived in Warsaw, and there were a lot of organizations.
And when they find out what's happening to the Jews in the camps-- not only camps, but just they're killing the Jews, so they see no end, no hope.
This was the only way to do something about it, is it resist.
They had bought some ammunition, some revolvers or some-- whatever you could on the [INAUDIBLE] on the other side, on the Polish side.
So they contacted the Polish side, and some stuff came in to some-- to be able to start the resistance.
They thought maybe by starting a resistant, maybe the outside world would do something.
We were not protected at all.
Could do nothing about it.
The Germans came with heavy armory, and the result-- shooting on the buildings and put fires all over.
[INAUDIBLE].
Yes.

As I said, they were coming through the building, and everybody was called go down, and they took the people out to the railroads, and they send them away, and marching the people to the railroad, and they took them away by the trains.
And we never heard of them.
We have-- as I said, through the building-- we couldn't go out of the building for the eight days.
After the eight days, the Germans came, and they said, everybody down.
And we came down, and they took us to the railroad.
Very simple.
And they took us to a concentration camp.
The first camp was Poniatow.
Poniatow.
P-O-N-I-A-T-O-W.
We were there by a couple weeks only.
Then they took us out of this camp, to another camp, Dorohucha, D-O-R-O-H-U-C-H-A.
Dorohucha.
There, we were working on the [? turf, ?] cutting [? turf, ?] so using it as coal for the building.
It was out and the winter comes, the wintertime, so you used this coal-- coal.

And from Dorohucha, being there a couple months seems like the SS send a message to the Dorohucha Germans and all over to Poland and all other camps that the people who worked in Warsaw in the printing-- they have a list of 39 people-- that time-- no, that time was already 120 people in the printing-- on the end.
And on this list were now our names, and they would call out these names in Dorohucha.
And they took us out, me and my wife, and a couple more people were brought in this-- were in our group among the workers of this printing in Warsaw.
And of this group, I don't remember how many there were, maybe eight or 10 people.
They send us to Lublin.
This was the place where of all camps, the people who worked and the printing supposed to come together.
And then we all came together after-- it took another week or so.
They took us-- the train-- to Radom, where the printing is, the new printing.
All the people from other camps came together in this place.
Yes.
In all the camps and all the place, we were together till later on, when the Russians were marching-- marching to Lublin.
They closed the printing in Radom.
They took us on the way to Auschwitz.
Radom was a small place, and we had an apartment outside the printing.
We went to morning to work, and evening we went to sleep in the apartment.
There was no life in Radom, no community life.
That's all to it.
Oh, we printed all these stuff for the Germans.
Police-- orders, you know, the-- not to shoot-- shoot so many people.
You know.
How's it called?
[INAUDIBLE] publications.
No, no, no.
How do you call it?
The bills-- billboards of the German.
And for the officers, the German officers, the printing we needed and the envelopes and the shoes and all kinds of printing.
Yeah.
I know-- as a matter of fact, I met him at his printing.
He was taken out from another camp.
He was taken out from a Lublin camp.
How is it called?
Majdanek.
He and his wife were in Majdanek.
He was on the list.
The wife was not.
The wife was working in a pharmacy, so she was not on the list.
But he came the same time with-- with us to Radom, and there we met.
Was a linotyper, and I was compositor [INAUDIBLE] and other things.
You know.
So he was alone, and I was with my wife.
For me, it was easier, everything.
He was lost, so we took him in in our group, and we tried to do whatever we could for him.
In [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he reported that Vladka Meed came to town, and that the Jews of Radom were frightened of her because they didn't know who she really was.
And how did the Melcers fit into that scene?
What did--
Well, so we-- we knew him from Warsaw as a neighbor-- her husband, but she came, and she had our names.
She saw the girl.

She saw our baby in New York in-- in Warsaw and she brought us money from the underground and documents for me and for my wife to try to get out-- I mean, to run away from the place, in case I have to.
We took the money, and we divided to needy people.
We know who-- who didn't have the money to buy extra food, you know what I mean?
Because we were in Radom, but a lot of people from Radom still in the ghetto, and some of the people of Radom started working in the printing.
[INAUDIBLE] were never printers.
We have to cover and say that he's a printer, and we took him in.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I said, she came, and she brought us the documents and money, and we divided the money.
We told everybody where the money comes from, and that's it.
How was Vladka Meed able to travel around?
She looked Polish, and she took a chance, of course.
Well, she arranged-- she told the people where we are.
So the woman, Janina Jamiolkowsky, took the baby.
As a matter of fact, she had her mother living the same city, Radom.
But she went with the baby to Radom, and in the front of the building, she was-- my baby was standing near the window where I walk inside.
And the-- but she and the baby was outside, and we saw the baby this way.
She was walking back and forth on the street where the printing was.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, nobody knew that she is visiting us, because we were afraid to tell anybody.
After Radom, where--
Because as I said, the Russian army took Lublin  that time.
It was not far away from Radom, was about 100 miles or something.
So the printer closed, and we took everybody from the printing, and we on the march by foot to Auschwitz.
On the way, a lot of people got killed, couldn't walk so much.
And then, on the end, they put us on a train.
We walked to Tomaszow, and in Tomaszow, we picked up a train to Auschwitz.
Oh, terrible conditions.
We were very crowded.
It was also a cattle train.
And I don't know how we went-- there were a day or two took us to travel.
No food, nothing until we came to Auschwitz.
No.
I mean, we were-- we couldn't move.
Everything-- wagon was closed up.
Well, when we came to Auschwitz, some-- we had a couple deaths on the train.
Some of the-- I think a friend of mine died in the same wagon.
I don't remember exactly, but he was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a good friend of ours, but he was a little, sickly man, and he died on the travel to-- travel to Auschwitz.
And I remember we-- we came to Auschwitz, and they're saying, out!
We went out, and we put in a line, line up everything.
And then this guy, Mengele, was in front of us, and he did the selection, right and left.
Whoever he did like, he put on this side, and we didn't know which is the right side.
Some people tried to switch, you know what I mean?
And it just happened that I was on the right side to wife-- I mean to transport, this was the line.
And the next day, we were transported to Germany as part of the transport, I mean.
Were you processed in-- in Auschwitz?
That's what I said.
This was the process, left, right.
Yes.
We went through the showers.
No.
That one day, no.
The people who left, who stayed in Auschwitz, they get the-- got the numbers because we were on transport to Germany.
My wife was there.
Donat was all the time with me, and my brother-in-law was all the time with me.
Kotlar.
Adas.
Adas, A-D-A-S.
Nothing, no, just-- she-- she went-- she went another line.
The women were separated.
They got out of the wagon, and we were separated.
I couldn't see anything.
I didn't know what happened to her.
No.
We were only one day in this camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then, next day, we went another train and took us to Germany.
It took us to Germany next day.
The place-- the first place was Hessental.
It's near Stuttgart.
Everything's near south Germany.
[INAUDIBLE]
I remember we worked there with-- cutting stones or something, that's all.
We were there for a couple of weeks, and then they took us to Vaihingen.
Vaihingen.
V-A-H-I-H-I-N-G-E-N, Vaihingen.
It was a working camp.
I went every day to work in the morning, all kind of jobs, especially they brought-- the last time, we worked in the underground factory.
They were building a underground factory because, you know, the airplanes used to come over.
British airplanes came over all the time and bombarded the-- all around this place.
And to end this, we were working in the factory at night.
It was a night shift.
During the night, British airplanes came in and bombarded the hole where the factory was supposed to be built, but the Germans run out.
They took place in special-- the places safe-- for safety.
And we were to stand out in the-- in the hole and watch that airplanes coming and bombarding the place.
This was the worst place in camp, where-- this place.
Then, we were transported.
They took us out from this camp.
This was when the American army start coming into Germany-- into Germany from the west.
So we were transported to Dachau, Allach, Dachau.
Dachau is near Munich.
It's about 20 miles, I think, from Munich.
In Dachau, we didn't work.
This was-- Dachau was Allach, Dachau.
Close to-- ?] Part of Dachau.
We did not work.
We didn't go out.
We stayed all the time in camp.
No-- no routine.
We were all the time in camp.
Oh, in-- in the morning, you have to get up, appell, of course.
And then, they didn't have no work for us.
They had in the mornings-- I don't remember exactly-- water, tea, sometimes something to it.
And-- we didn't eat too much.
We were all hungry, very hungry.
The barracks-- we sleep in the barrack, on the floor with-- with hay in it, whatever we have.
No.
Nothing.
I never worked after this, really after Germany-- after the printing.
I never did the right work.
I have to work other things like everybody else.
With stone, cutting stone, carrying this, carrying that.
No.
A couple hundred.
Big barracks.
We didn't have too many friends there.
I mean, everybody was for himself.
But there was nothing to do.
All you have to do is sit in bed and doing nothing.
Some groups went out to work.
They took them out to work.
I don't remember.
They took us out to the work in-- in Allach.
It was about the end of the-- of the war.
You know, we came to Allach probably in March, so we stayed a couple of weeks there.
And at the end, when the-- the American army was near, they took out the people from Allach.
And I don't know how it happened.
They left a part of the people in Allach, and I was the part who stayed in Allach.
The other part-- most people went on a march by foot to Garmisch-Partenkirchen, to the Alps by foot, and they were liberated there.
On the way, some were killed too.
And the place where I stayed, Allach, was bombarded by the American army.
It took us two, three days.
They bombed with a little fight with the Germans, probably.
But we were standing in-- under the table, hiding in the barracks, so-- because of bombardment all around.
And then the German-- the Americans came in.
We saw no more Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was May 1.
We're liberated.
You couldn't get sick.
If you're sick, you go.
Yes.
As a matter of fact, I was sick, and I had typhus.
And Donat was in that camp.
He was in Vaihingen.
In Vaihingen, Donat was a sanitary.
And he helped me when I had the typhus.
I was taken into this place.
It was not a hospital, just a separated-- a separate barrack of the sick people, and he helped me [INAUDIBLE].
Not really.
No.
Sometimes it was random.
Sometimes it was something-- they didn't like this.
The German-- something didn't stay straight.
They didn't do this, or they didn't do that.
Some Germans like to punish.
I don't remember, no.
It was a-- how is it called?
Every group has a leader, and he was responsible.
A kapo, they called.
